_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Memory Ownership practice 1
questions.prompt: What does `std::move` do to a unique_ptr?
choices: Transfers its ownership to the destination
Copies the owned object automatically
Deletes both pointers
Makes the object global
questions.explanation: Move transfers the unique ownership; the destination owns the object and the source becomes empty.

questions.title: Memory Ownership practice 2
choices: When several parts truly share ownership
For every local integer
Whenever a raw reference is enough
To avoid including memory
questions.explanation: A shared pointer models multiple owners whose lifetimes overlap, not ordinary temporary access to an object.

questions.title: Trace Memory Ownership
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The unique pointer owns the Student, move transfers that ownership into active, and cleanup happens automatically when active leaves scope. The resulting output is 'Ada'.

questions.title: Memory Ownership practice 5
questions.prompt: What happens to the Student when `student` (a unique_ptr) goes out of scope?
choices: It is destroyed automatically, with no manual delete needed
It leaks unless you call delete student
It is copied, so two Students now exist
It stays alive until the program ends
questions.explanation: A `unique_ptr` owns its object and deletes it when the pointer leaves scope. That is the reason to prefer it over raw `new`: the cleanup is tied to the object's lifetime, so there is no `delete` to forget. A unique_ptr cannot be copied, and the object does not outlive its owner.

questions.title: Memory Ownership concept check 1
questions.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "Shared pointer is not the default"?
choices: Copies the owned object automatically

`std::shared_ptr` uses a reference count and is appropriate when ownership is genuinely shared. Using it everywhere can hide unclear lifetime design and can leak cycles, so begin with values, containers, or unique ownership.
Deletes both pointers
Makes the object global
questions.explanation: Prefer objects with automatic storage and standard containers. When one owner needs dynamic lifetime, use `std::unique_ptr` with `std::make_unique`. That reasoning supports the answer: `std::shared_ptr` uses a reference count and is appropriate when ownership is genuinely shared. Using it everywhere can hide unclear lifetime design and can leak cycles, so begin with values, containers, or unique ownership.

questions.title: Memory Ownership concept check 2
questions.prompt: Which choice describes a reliable Memory Ownership practice?
choices: For every local integer
Whenever a raw reference is enough
Prefer values and standard containers first.
To avoid including memory
questions.explanation: Prefer objects with automatic storage and standard containers. When one owner needs dynamic lifetime, use `std::unique_ptr` with `std::make_unique`. That reasoning supports the answer: Prefer values and standard containers first.

questions.title: Memory Ownership concept check 3
questions.prompt: Which recommendation belongs in a review of Memory Ownership code?
choices: Makes the object global
For every local integer
Use make_unique for one dynamic owner.
questions.explanation: Prefer objects with automatic storage and standard containers. When one owner needs dynamic lifetime, use `std::unique_ptr` with `std::make_unique`. That reasoning supports the answer: Use make_unique for one dynamic owner.

questions.title: Memory Ownership concept check 4
questions.prompt: Which guideline helps prevent a common Memory Ownership mistake?
choices: Move unique ownership deliberately.
Whenever a raw reference is enough
To avoid including memory
questions.explanation: Prefer objects with automatic storage and standard containers. When one owner needs dynamic lifetime, use `std::unique_ptr` with `std::make_unique`. That reasoning supports the answer: Move unique ownership deliberately.

questions.title: Memory Ownership concept check 5
questions.prompt: A classmate is checking their understanding of Memory Ownership. Which advice is correct?
choices: Copies the owned object automatically
Use shared ownership only when the model requires it.
Deletes both pointers
Whenever a raw reference is enough
questions.explanation: Prefer objects with automatic storage and standard containers. When one owner needs dynamic lifetime, use `std::unique_ptr` with `std::make_unique`. That reasoning supports the answer: Use shared ownership only when the model requires it.

questions.title: Memory Ownership concept check 6
questions.prompt: What is the main idea behind Memory Ownership?
choices: To avoid including memory
Use automatic lifetime and smart pointers to make resource responsibility clear.
Copies the owned object automatically
questions.explanation: Prefer objects with automatic storage and standard containers. When one owner needs dynamic lifetime, use `std::unique_ptr` with `std::make_unique`. That reasoning supports the answer: Use automatic lifetime and smart pointers to make resource responsibility clear.
